title: Selection, Dosing, and Monitoring of Steroid Replacement in the Patient With Adrenal Failure
authors: Irina Bancos, MD; Richard Auchus, MD, PhD

items.title: Why dexamethasone should not be used for adrenal insufficiency replacement
items.text: Its long half-life prevents physiologic circadian replacement, and the smallest available tablet potency commonly causes overtreatment and iatrogenic Cushing syndrome with limited dose-titration flexibility.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Dexamethasone should not be used in the treatment of adrenal insufficiency for several reasons. It has a long half-life, which does not allow for diurnal replacement."

items.title: How to initiate hydrocortisone replacement in adrenal insufficiency
items.text: Typical total daily dose is 15–25 mg in 2–3 divided doses mimicking the cortisol nadir and peaks. Prednisone/prednisolone 4–6 mg once each morning is an alternative when once-daily dosing is preferred.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Hydrocortisone is the most commonly used glucocorticoid, with a starting dosage of 15 to 25 mg daily in divided (2 to 3) doses."

items.title: Why mineralocorticoid replacement is required in primary but not secondary adrenal insufficiency
items.text: Primary adrenal failure destroys the zona glomerulosa; ~95% of patients need fludrocortisone. Secondary AI retains aldosterone secretion via the renin–angiotensin system.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Mineralocorticoid replacement therapy consists of fludrocortisone, 100 mcg daily (usually 50-300 mcg daily), and is required in 95% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency."

items.title: Clinical monitoring of glucocorticoid replacement
items.text: Assess weight, energy, sleep, sick-day steroid use, and crisis history. Routine bloodwork is not mandatory, though post-dose cortisol can confirm exposure. Overtreatment causes weight gain and cushingoid features; undertreatment causes fatigue, nausea, and crisis risk.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Monitoring of glucocorticoid replacement therapy includes history and physical examination (Table 2)."

items.title: How to approach DHEA replacement in women with adrenal insufficiency
items.text: Consider a trial of DHEA 25 mg daily in symptomatic women (especially low libido/fatigue) after stabilizing glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid therapy; target mid-normal DHEA-S and reassess at ≥6 months.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Androgen replacement therapy has been described in women with either primary or secondary adrenal insufficiency with a starting dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) dosage of 25 mg daily."

items.title: Monitoring fludrocortisone therapy
items.text: Track orthostatic blood pressure, edema, potassium, sodium, and renin (or renin plasma activity) at least yearly or 3 months after dose changes. Goal: no orthostasis and renin near normal.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Monitoring includes assessment of orthostatic vitals during each visit, electrolytes, and renin plasma activity at least yearly or 3 months after each dosage adjustment (Table 2)."

items.title: Why sick-day education prevents adrenal crisis
items.text: Even educated patients have high crisis rates during intercurrent illness. Doubling oral glucocorticoid during febrile illness and switching to parenteral steroids when vomiting or severely ill are lifesaving interventions.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "As such, comprehensive education, instruction on injection, and provision of proper supplies are important interventions for adrenal crisis prevention (Table 4)."

items.title: How to adjust glucocorticoids during pregnancy in adrenal insufficiency
items.text: Increase hydrocortisone ~20% in the second and third trimesters (example: 15 mg → 25 mg then 30 mg daily) and monitor whether progesterone's antimineralocorticoid effect requires fludrocortisone adjustment late in pregnancy.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Increase the hydrocortisone to 25 mg daily in the second trimester and to 30 mg daily in the third trimester; monitor the need for fludrocortisone adjustment"

items.title: Glucocorticoid timing for shift workers
items.text: Align the larger hydrocortisone dose with the patient's waking period and give the second dose 6–8 hours later, adjusting mineralocorticoid timing similarly.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Shift worker: Change glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement according to schedule (take the larger dose of hydrocortisone on waking, the second dose 6 to 8 hours after the first one)"

items.title: Why cosyntropin stimulation can miss secondary adrenal insufficiency
items.text: The high-dose (250 mcg) ACTH test has suboptimal sensitivity for secondary AI, yielding false negatives when the adrenal cortex is atrophied but still responds to supraphysiologic ACTH.
items.reference: Case 4: "it is important to remember suboptimal sensitivity of cosyntropin-stimulation testing in diagnosing secondary adrenal insufficiency (high number of false-negative results)."

items.title: How to structure adrenal crisis prevention for every patient
items.text: Provide steroid emergency identification, teach sick-day rules (double oral dose during illness; inject and seek emergency care if unable to take oral steroids), and ensure injectable glucocorticoid supplies at home.
items.reference: Table 4. Adrenal Crisis Prevention: "Sick day rule 2: inject glucocorticoid in times of inability to take oral glucocorticoid or severe illness AND proceed to emergency care"

items.title: Steroid replacement adjustments in end-stage renal disease
items.text: Stop fludrocortisone on dialysis and anticipate loss of morning hydrocortisone during hemodialysis—plan dosing with the nephrology team.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Dialysis: Stop fludrocortisone (no longer needed) Plan glucocorticoid replacement (substantial amounts of the morning glucocorticoid dose might be lost through hemodialysis)"

items.question: A 31-year-old woman with autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency is 8 weeks pregnant on hydrocortisone 15 mg AM + 5 mg noon and fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily. She is well with normal electrolytes and renin at goal. What additional adjustment is recommended?
options: No changes throughout pregnancy
Double hydrocortisone in the third trimester only
Increase hydrocortisone to 25 mg daily in the second trimester and 30 mg daily in the third; monitor fludrocortisone
Increase both hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone by 20% immediately
items.explanation: Physiologic cortisol demand rises in later pregnancy; typical practice increases hydrocortisone in the second and third trimesters and reassesses mineralocorticoid need as progesterone antagonizes aldosterone.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Increase the hydrocortisone to 25 mg daily in the second trimester and to 30 mg daily in the third trimester; monitor the need for fludrocortisone adjustment"

items.question: A 23-year-old woman with primary adrenal insufficiency has afternoon fatigue and insomnia on hydrocortisone 15 mg on waking and 5 mg at bedtime, with otherwise adequate replacement. What change is best?
options: Refer to sleep medicine without medication changes
Add 5 mg hydrocortisone at noon only
Move bedtime hydrocortisone to early afternoon and consider DHEA 25 mg trial
Switch to dexamethasone 0.5 mg at bedtime
items.explanation: Bedtime hydrocortisone can disrupt sleep; moving dose earlier mimics circadian physiology. DHEA may help libido/fatigue in young women after glucocorticoid timing is optimized.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Trial of DHEA supplementation AND move the bedtime hydrocortisone dose to the early afternoon"

items.question: A panhypopituitary man after bilateral adrenalectomy for Cushing disease asks about continuous subcutaneous hydrocortisone via insulin pump. What should you tell him?
options: Subcutaneous hydrocortisone is clearly superior for quality of life
Hydrocortisone is not FDA approved for SC infusion and patient satisfaction data are mixed
SC and oral hydrocortisone exposure are milligram-for-milligram identical
Pumps eliminate all risk of adrenal crisis
items.explanation: Evidence is limited and heterogeneous; hydrocortisone is not FDA approved for SC infusion, and patient preference varies.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: E) Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is not FDA approved for subcutaneous infusion AND the data regarding patient satisfaction with subcutaneous hydrocortisone are mixed"

items.question: A 35-year-old man on tramadol 400 mg daily has morning cortisol 3 μg/dL, low DHEA-S, ACTH 9 pg/mL, but peak cortisol 18 μg/dL after 250 mcg cosyntropin. Pituitary MRI a year ago was normal. Best next step?
options: Reassure him adrenal insufficiency is excluded
Perform 1-mcg cosyntropin test
Repeat pituitary MRI urgently
Evaluate for opioid-induced central hypogonadism and manage adrenal insufficiency
items.explanation: Long-term opioids cause secondary AI with false-negative high-dose cosyntropin tests; central hypogonadism is the most common associated endocrinopathy and should be evaluated.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: D) Test for central hypogonadism"

items.question: A 40-year-old teacher with well-controlled primary adrenal insufficiency asks about COVID-19 risk and steroid adjustments. What advice is correct?
options: She meets CDC immunosuppressed criteria on her hydrocortisone dose
Begin stress dosing now because of workplace exposure
Review sick-day rules and ensure ample hydrocortisone plus an alternative glucocorticoid prescription
Refuse dexamethasone if hospitalized for COVID-19 pneumonia
items.explanation: She is not immunosuppressed by CDC steroid thresholds, but infection remains a crisis trigger—reinforce sick-day rules and medication supply redundancy.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: C) She should review sick-day rules and ensure that she has access to an ample supply of hydrocortisone and an alternative glucocorticoid"

items.question: Which glucocorticoid is least appropriate for chronic replacement in primary adrenal insufficiency?
options: Hydrocortisone 20 mg/day divided
Prednisone 5 mg each morning
Prednisolone 5 mg each morning
Dexamethasone 0.25 mg each morning
items.explanation: Dexamethasone's potency and long half-life make physiologic replacement and titration difficult.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Dexamethasone should not be used in the treatment of adrenal insufficiency for several reasons."

items.question: A patient with new primary adrenal insufficiency has orthostatic dizziness and hyperkalemia on hydrocortisone alone. Typical starting fludrocortisone dose?
options: 25 mcg daily
100 mcg daily
1 mg daily
No mineralocorticoid is ever needed
items.explanation: Standard starting dose is 100 mcg daily, titrated by blood pressure, edema, electrolytes, and renin.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Mineralocorticoid replacement therapy consists of fludrocortisone, 100 mcg daily"

items.question: A patient with adrenal insufficiency develops fever and gastroenteritis but can swallow fluids. What glucocorticoid adjustment is appropriate?
options: Continue usual dose until recovered
Double the daily oral glucocorticoid dose until well for 24 hours
Stop hydrocortisone to avoid Cushing syndrome
Switch permanently to twice the maintenance dose
items.explanation: Sick-day rule 1: double daily oral glucocorticoid during intercurrent illness until recovered for one day.
items.reference: Table 4. Adrenal Crisis Prevention: "Sick day rule 1: double daily glucocorticoid dose in times of illness until well for 1 day"

items.question: A patient with primary adrenal insufficiency and insulin-dependent diabetes has wide glucose excursions on divided hydrocortisone. What adjustment may help?
options: Switch to once-daily prednisolone to smooth peaks
Stop all glucocorticoids
Replace fludrocortisone with dexamethasone
Use only mineralocorticoid
items.explanation: Longer-acting morning prednisolone may reduce cortisol-driven glucose peaks compared with short-acting divided hydrocortisone.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus: Consider longer-acting glucocorticoids (eg, prednisolone) to avoid cortisol-driven peaks and troughs in blood glucose"

items.question: A patient with primary adrenal insufficiency starts hemodialysis. What change to steroid therapy is indicated?
options: Increase fludrocortisone to 200 mcg
Discontinue fludrocortisone and coordinate morning hydrocortisone with dialysis timing
Add dexamethasone nightly
No changes ever needed on dialysis
items.explanation: Fludrocortisone is not needed on dialysis; morning hydrocortisone may be lost in the dialysate and requires planning.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Dialysis: Stop fludrocortisone (no longer needed)"

items.question: A patient with primary adrenal insufficiency plans prolonged work in a hot climate. What adjustment should be considered?
options: Stop fludrocortisone to prevent hypertension
Additional salt intake and possible fludrocortisone increase for sweat losses
Switch to dexamethasone for heat stability
Halve hydrocortisone to reduce sweating
items.explanation: Heat increases salt/water losses; additional salt and sometimes higher fludrocortisone compensate.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Hot environment: Additional salt requirement to replace sweat losses Consider increasing fludrocortisone by 50 to 100 mg daily to compensate for salt and water loss"

items.question: According to Table 1, which daily regimen approximates 20 mg hydrocortisone equivalent?
options: Prednisone 5 mg once daily
Cortisone acetate 30–40 mg daily
Methylprednisolone 16 mg daily
Dexamethasone 1 mg daily
items.explanation: Prednisone 5 mg ≈ 20 mg hydrocortisone equivalent per the chapter potency table.
items.reference: Table 1. Glucocorticoid Type and Potency: "Prednisone 0.25 Hydrocortisone Equivalent ... Total Daily Dose 5 mg"

items.question: Which scenario most commonly precipitates adrenal crisis in patients with chronic adrenal insufficiency?
options: Mild seasonal allergies
Viral gastroenteritis or upper respiratory infection
Routine dental cleaning without stress dosing
Single missed noon hydrocortisone dose while well
items.explanation: Intercurrent infection—especially GI illness with vomiting—is the classic crisis trigger; education and injectable supplies are critical.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Adrenal crisis usually occurs during a viral illness, such as gastroenteritis or an upper respiratory infection."

items.statement: Dexamethasone is preferred for adrenal insufficiency because its long half-life simplifies once-daily dosing.
items.explanation: Long half-life prevents physiologic circadian replacement and commonly causes overtreatment.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Dexamethasone should not be used in the treatment of adrenal insufficiency for several reasons."

items.statement: Fludrocortisone is required in approximately 95% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Primary adrenal cortex destruction impairs aldosterone production in most patients.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "is required in 95% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency."

items.statement: Routine blood tests are mandatory to monitor stable glucocorticoid replacement in adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Clinical assessment predominates; labs are optional though post-dose cortisol can confirm exposure.
items.reference: Table 2. Monitoring Steroid Replacement Therapy: "No bloodwork is needed for monitoring of therapy, but can measure post-dose cortisol to confirm exposure"

items.statement: DHEA replacement in women with adrenal insufficiency may improve fatigue, mood, and libido.
items.explanation: Meta-analyses show modest but statistically significant quality-of-life benefits, greater in younger women.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "small but statistically significant improvement in quality of life, fatigue, mood, and libido in women treated with DHEA"

items.statement: A normal 250-mcg cosyntropin test reliably excludes secondary adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: High false-negative rate in secondary AI is a recognized pitfall, especially with opioid use.
items.reference: Case 4: "suboptimal sensitivity of cosyntropin-stimulation testing in diagnosing secondary adrenal insufficiency (high number of false-negative results)."

items.statement: Plasma renin is a reliable marker of mineralocorticoid adequacy throughout pregnancy.
items.explanation: Renin is physiologically increased in pregnancy and is not useful for monitoring fludrocortisone.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "Monitor with sodium and potassium (plasma renin is physiologically increased in pregnancy and thus not useful)"

items.statement: A patient on hydrocortisone 15 mg daily meets the CDC definition of immunosuppressed.
items.explanation: CDC threshold is ≥20 mg prednisone equivalent (~80–100 mg hydrocortisone).
items.reference: Case 5: "The Centers for Disease Control definition of immunosuppressed, however, is taking at least 20 mg daily of prednisone or equivalent (80-100 mg of hydrocortisone)"

items.statement: In adrenal insufficiency with hypertension on adequate fludrocortisone, calcium-channel blockers are reasonable antihypertensives.
items.explanation: Calcium-channel blockers and alpha-blockers are preferred; ACE inhibitors/ARBs are less effective.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "effective antihypertensive agents include calcium-channel blockers and α-adrenergic blockers"

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone should be given in divided daily doses for adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Reason: Physiologic cortisol secretion follows a circadian rhythm with a morning peak.
items.explanation: Both are true and the circadian physiology explains divided dosing.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Glucocorticoid potency and circadian delivery need to be considered when deciding on the glucocorticoid type, dose, and frequency of administration."

items.assertion: Assertion: Fludrocortisone is not routinely required in secondary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Reason: Secondary adrenal insufficiency always abolishes aldosterone secretion.
items.explanation: Assertion is true, but aldosterone is largely preserved in secondary AI—the reason is false.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Mineralocorticoid replacement therapy consists of fludrocortisone, 100 mcg daily (usually 50-300 mcg daily), and is required in 95% of patients with primary adrenal insufficiency."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone dose should increase during the second and third trimesters of pregnancy in adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Reason: Placental CRH drives adrenal cortisol hyperplasia and higher cortisol requirements.
items.explanation: Both are true; pregnancy increases physiologic cortisol demand.
items.reference: Case 1: "Cortisol production increases during pregnancy, possibly due to increased placental production of corticotropin-releasing hormone"

items.assertion: Assertion: Chronic opioid therapy can cause secondary adrenal insufficiency with a false-negative cosyntropin test.
items.reason: Reason: Opioids stimulate adrenal cortisol production via CRH hypersecretion.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; opioids suppress the HPA axis—the stated stimulatory reason is false.
items.reference: Case 4: "Opioid-induced adrenal insufficiency should be considered in this patient due to long-term use of tramadol"

items.assertion: Assertion: DHEA should be initiated only after glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid therapy are stabilized.
items.explanation: Assertion reflects best practice; DHEA is androgen replacement, not mineralocorticoid.
items.reference: Glucocorticoid Replacement Therapy: "Another approach is to discuss DHEA with every woman who has adrenal insufficiency after stabilization of glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid therapy."

items.assertion: Assertion: Patients unable to take oral glucocorticoids during severe illness should receive injectable steroids and emergency care.
items.reason: Reason: Continued oral absorption is guaranteed during vomiting.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; vomiting prevents reliable oral absorption—the reason is false.
items.reference: Table 4. Adrenal Crisis Prevention: "Sick day rule 2: inject glucocorticoid in times of inability to take oral glucocorticoid or severe illness AND proceed to emergency care"

items.assertion: Assertion: Continuous subcutaneous hydrocortisone is not FDA approved.
items.reason: Reason: No study has ever administered hydrocortisone subcutaneously.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; studies exist but FDA approval for SC infusion is lacking—the reason is false.
items.reference: Case 3: "Hydrocortisone is not FDA approved for subcutaneous infusion (Answer B)"

items.assertion: Assertion: Extra hydrocortisone doses may be needed during prolonged strenuous exercise.
items.reason: Reason: Marathon-level exertion increases cortisol requirements and salt losses.
items.explanation: Both are true; the chapter advises small supplemental doses and salt during extreme exercise.
items.reference: Table 3. Special Circumstances in Steroid Replacement Therapy: "consider 2.5 to 5 mg hydrocortisone every 3 hours during a marathon run or triathlon"
